relevant_files.role: Core regex-based pattern engine that compiles 14 security patterns and scans text line-by-line
SecurityScanner orchestrates scanning of PR diffs and files, parses unified diff format, tracks line numbers
Type definitions for Finding, PatternDefinition, Severity, Confidence, ValidationResult
needs_security_scan() function determines whether to trigger scans based on PR labels, keywords, file paths
14 regex patterns for hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, XSS, path traversal, weak crypto, unsafe deserialization
Integration tests including test_multi_line_vulnerability_not_detected() documenting known limitation
Performance benchmarks validating <10ms scanning on 500-line code samples
SecurityConfig for ignore rules on patterns and paths
Dependencies: regex, serde, reqwest, tokio, thiserror, anyhow

conventions.commits: Conventional commits with GPG sign and DCO sign-off (git commit -S --signoff); feature branches only, all changes via PR
conventions.testing: AAA pattern (Arrange, Act, Assert); unit tests in mod.rs, integration tests in tests/ directory, benchmarks in benches/; one happy path + one edge case per behavior
conventions.linting: cargo clippy -- -D warnings, cargo fmt --check, cargo test, cargo deny check advisories licenses
conventions.error_handling: thiserror for libraries (Finding, PatternDefinition errors), anyhow for applications; Result<T> return types throughout

patterns: Line-by-line regex scanning with LazyLock compiled patterns
Unified diff parsing to track file names and line numbers
Integration with LLM validator for false positive reduction
Ignore rules for paths and pattern IDs

related_issues.title: Document multi-line vulnerability limitation
related_issues.relevance: Directly motivates tree-sitter evaluation; test_multi_line_vulnerability_not_detected() documents the gap

related_issues.title: feat(security): evaluate tree-sitter for AST-based vulnerability detection
related_issues.relevance: Target issue; proposes hybrid regex+tree-sitter approach for 500-800 LOC, 14 pattern conversions, starting with Rust SQL injection

constraints: Multi-crate workspace (aptu-core is primary security module)
Regex patterns must maintain backward compatibility with existing 14 patterns
Performance requirement: <10ms for 500-line code samples (from benchmarks)
Must integrate with existing SecurityValidator (LLM-based false positive filtering)
Pattern definitions stored in embedded JSON; changing storage format is breaking change
Unified diff parsing is core to PR scanning workflow
File extension filtering per pattern (e.g., SQL injection only for .rs, .py, .js, .ts, .java, .php)
test_coverage: Integration tests in security_integration.rs cover: hardcoded secrets detection, SQL injection detection (concat+format), safe patterns (zero findings), multi-line vulnerability limitation. Benchmarks in security_scan.rs validate performance on 500-line safe and vulnerable code samples. Unit tests in patterns.rs, scanner.rs, types.rs, detection.rs, ignore.rs cover individual components. Known gap: multi-line patterns explicitly documented as not detected (issue #735).

library_findings.relevant_api: Language grammars (tree_sitter_rust, tree_sitter_python, etc.), Query API for pattern matching on AST nodes, tree traversal with cursor
library_findings.notes: Provides AST parsing for 50+ languages; Query language enables semantic pattern matching (e.g., find all function calls with string concatenation). No Rust crate in current Cargo.toml; would require adding tree-sitter and language-specific grammar crates.

Current implementation uses LazyLock<Regex> for compiled patterns. Regex crate is mature and performant for line-by-line matching but cannot match across line boundaries without buffering entire file.
Used to deserialize patterns.json into Vec<PatternDefinition>. Any AST-based approach must preserve JSON pattern format or provide migration path.

approaches.name: Hybrid Regex+Tree-Sitter with Gradual Migration
approaches.description: Keep regex engine as default, add optional tree-sitter backend for specific patterns (SQL injection, command injection). Create new TreeSitterEngine struct alongside PatternEngine. Patterns get optional 'ast_query' field in JSON. SecurityScanner routes patterns to appropriate engine based on availability. Start with Rust SQL injection (tree-sitter-rust grammar), expand to other languages incrementally.
pros: Zero breaking changes to existing regex patterns
Allows A/B testing regex vs tree-sitter on same patterns
Reduces scope: only convert patterns that benefit from AST (multi-line, semantic)
cons: Adds complexity: dual pattern engines, routing logic, two dependency trees
Requires maintaining both regex and AST versions of patterns
JSON schema change (optional 'ast_query' field) needs migration
Performance overhead of choosing engine per pattern
Testing burden doubles for patterns with both engines

approaches.name: Full Tree-Sitter Replacement with Pattern Rewrite
approaches.description: Replace PatternEngine entirely with TreeSitterEngine. Rewrite all 14 patterns as tree-sitter Query strings (one per language grammar). Store queries in patterns.json with language-specific variants. Extend PatternDefinition to include 'queries' map (language -> query string). SecurityScanner detects file language, parses AST, runs queries. Regex fallback for unsupported languages.
pros: Query language is more expressive than regex for semantic patterns
Better false positive reduction (e.g., only flag actual SQL execution calls)
Scales to new languages easily (just add grammar + queries)
cons: Large breaking change: all 14 patterns must be rewritten
Dependency on external grammar crates (tree-sitter-rust, tree-sitter-python, etc.)
Performance risk: AST parsing slower than regex for large files
Regex fallback adds complexity; must maintain both code paths

approaches.name: Spike: Tree-Sitter for SQL Injection Only (Proof of Concept)
approaches.description: Add tree-sitter-rust as optional dependency. Create new module security/tree_sitter_engine.rs with single function detect_sql_injection_ast(code: &str) -> Vec<Finding>. Implement tree-sitter Query to find string concatenation in function calls (e.g., query() + user_input). Keep existing regex engine untouched. Add integration test comparing regex vs tree-sitter results on multi-line SQL injection fixture. Document findings in issue #737 for decision on full migration.
pros: Minimal scope: ~200-300 LOC, one pattern, one language
Fast feedback loop: validate tree-sitter approach before full commitment
No breaking changes; existing code unaffected
Clear decision point: if spike succeeds, propose full migration
cons: Doesn't solve the full problem; only SQL injection in Rust
Spike code may be discarded (sunk effort)
Doesn't address other languages (Python, JS, etc.)
May create technical debt if not completed

recommendation: Approach 3 (Spike: Tree-Sitter for SQL Injection Only) is recommended as the first step. It directly addresses the issue's proposed spike approach, validates tree-sitter feasibility with concrete multi-line detection, and provides data to inform a full migration decision. The spike is low-risk (optional dependency, no breaking changes, ~300 LOC), aligns with issue #737's scope, and generates actionable feedback. Success criteria: (1) detect multi-line SQL injection in Rust that regex misses, (2) <10ms performance on 500-line samples, (3) zero false positives vs regex baseline. If spike succeeds, propose Approach 1 (Hybrid) as the migration path to avoid rewriting all 14 patterns at once. Approach 2 (Full Replacement) is the long-term vision but too risky for initial implementation.
